question: Do the parables conceal or reveal?

positions.label: Conceal-only view
positions.proponents: Matthew’s parallel says the parables are used because the people cannot understand. The function is primarily concealment from the resistant.
positions.argument: Concealment function

positions.label: Reveal-to-insiders view
positions.proponents: The parables reveal to those with open hearts while confirming the hardness of those who have already rejected. The same story functions differently based on the posture of the hearer.
positions.argument: Dual function

title: Did Jesus raise the dead or was she sleeping?
question: Did Jesus raise the dead or was she sleeping?

positions.label: Resurrection view
positions.argument: Resurrection

positions.label: Resuscitation view
positions.proponents: Some suggest the girl was in a deep coma and Jesus correctly diagnosed it. The crowd’s laughter (v.40) supports the possibility that her death was uncertain.
positions.argument: Resuscitation

title: Did Jesus physically walk on water?
question: Did Jesus physically walk on water?

positions.label: Historical view
positions.proponents: The event is recorded in three Gospels (Matt, Mark, John) with distinctive independent details. The disciples’ reaction (terror → worship) is consistent with genuine encounter with the unexpected.
positions.argument: Historical miracle

positions.label: Symbolic/theological view
positions.argument: Post-resurrection tradition

title: Was Jesus’s “dogs” remark genuinely harsh?
question: Was Jesus’s “dogs” remark genuinely harsh?

positions.label: Test-of-faith view
positions.proponents: Jesus knew the woman’s faith and was drawing it out. The diminutive kynarion (household dogs) is less insulting than the street-dog slur.
positions.argument: Pedagogical test

positions.label: Priority-of-Israel view
positions.proponents: Jesus genuinely maintained the covenant priority (Israel first) and only extended healing after the woman’s persistent faith demonstrated Gentile receptivity.
positions.argument: Real covenant order

title: Is Peter’s confession the Gospel’s structural centre?
question: Is Peter’s confession the Gospel’s structural centre?

positions.label: Centre-pivot view
positions.argument: Structural pivot

positions.label: Continuous-narrative view
positions.proponents: Mark’s Gospel should not be forced into a two-part structure. The material flows more organically without a sharp structural break at 8:29.
positions.argument: Continuous flow

title: Where was the Transfiguration?
question: Where was the Transfiguration?

positions.label: Mount Tabor
positions.proponents: Traditional site since Origen; a rounded hill in the Jezreel Valley, c.570m.
positions.argument: Traditional identification

positions.label: Mount Hermon
positions.proponents: Geographically more consistent: near Caesarea Philippi (six days earlier), at 2,814m genuinely high.
positions.argument: Geographical consistency

title: Is Mark 10:45 substitutionary atonement?
question: Is Mark 10:45 substitutionary atonement?

positions.label: Substitutionary view
positions.proponents: Anti pollōn (for many) uses the preposition of substitution. The lytron pays what the many owe. Jesus takes the place of those who are ransomed.
positions.argument: Substitutionary

positions.label: Liberation/Exodus view
positions.proponents: The ransom language draws on the Exodus pattern: YHWH ransomed Israel from Egypt without paying a price to Pharaoh. The liberation is real without requiring a payment to a captor.
positions.argument: Liberation

title: Was the Temple action a cleansing or a judgment?
question: Was the Temple action a cleansing or a judgment?

positions.label: Cleansing view
positions.proponents: Jesus restored the Temple’s proper function by removing commercial corruption from the Court of the Gentiles. The action is reformist.
positions.argument: Reformist cleansing

positions.label: Judgment view
positions.proponents: Jesus enacted the Temple’s coming destruction. The overturned tables are a prophetic sign, not a reform measure. E.P. Sanders argues this view.
positions.argument: Prophetic judgment

title: Is the widow’s offering praised or lamented?
question: Is the widow’s offering praised or lamented?

positions.label: Praise view
positions.proponents: Jesus commends the widow’s complete generosity as the model of covenant faithfulness. She gives more than the rich because she gives everything.
positions.argument: Commendation

positions.label: Lament view
positions.proponents: Jesus is lamenting that the Temple system has consumed this widow’s last resources, fulfilling 12:40 (“they devour widows’ houses”). Her giving is tragic, not exemplary.
positions.argument: Indictment of the system

title: Does “this generation” refer to AD 70 or to a future generation?
question: Does “this generation” refer to AD 70 or to a future generation?

positions.label: AD 70 view
positions.proponents: Genea consistently means the contemporary generation in Mark. The signs of vv.5-23 fit the Jewish War of AD 66-70. The prediction was fulfilled within a generation of Jesus’s lifetime.
positions.argument: Near-term fulfilment

positions.label: Future generation view
positions.proponents: “This generation” refers to the generation that sees the specific signs of vv.14-22 begin. The ultimate parousia (vv.24-27) was not fulfilled in AD 70.
positions.argument: Future eschatological generation

title: Was the Last Supper a Passover meal?
question: Was the Last Supper a Passover meal?

positions.label: Yes — Passover meal
positions.proponents: Mark 14:12-17 sets the meal on Nisan 14/15, when the Passover lamb was sacrificed. The Passover framework is explicit in the preparation narrative.
positions.argument: Passover meal

positions.label: No — the day before
positions.proponents: John 18:28 places the crucifixion on the Day of Preparation, meaning the Last Supper was the evening before Passover. Jesus dies as the lambs are being slaughtered in the Temple.
positions.argument: Pre-Passover meal

title: What did the torn veil signify?
question: What did the torn veil signify?

positions.label: Access to God
positions.argument: Access to the Most Holy Place

positions.label: Temple judgment
positions.proponents: The tearing is a sign of God’s judgment on the Temple system, anticipating its physical destruction in AD 70.
positions.argument: Judgment on the Temple

title: Where does the original Mark end?
question: Where does the original Mark end?

positions.label: 16:8 original ending
positions.argument: Original ending at 16:8

positions.label: 16:9-20 canonical ending
positions.proponents: The longer ending is included in the NT canon (Irenaeus quotes it as early as c.AD 185) and has been the church’s received text for most of Christian history.
positions.argument: Canonical longer ending

title: Is Mary's consent to the Annunciation essential to the Incarnation?
question: Was Mary's 'yes' to the angel essential for the Incarnation, or would God have accomplished it regardless?
context: When Gabriel announces that Mary will conceive the Messiah, she responds: 'I am the Lord's servant. May your word to me be fulfilled' (Luke 1:38). Catholic and Orthodox theology emphasizes Mary's fiat ('let it be done') as a free act of consent that cooperated with divine grace — the 'new Eve' whose obedience reverses the first Eve's disobedience. Reformed theology emphasizes divine sovereignty — God's plan cannot be contingent on human cooperation. The question touches the nature of grace, free will, and the relationship between divine sovereignty and human agency.

positions.label: Necessary consent view
positions.proponents: Mary’s "may your word to me be fulfilled" (1:38) is a genuine consent without which the Incarnation would not proceed. The Incarnation required a human yes.
positions.argument: Consent as condition
positions.strengths: The narrative presents a genuine dialogue — Gabriel explains, Mary questions, then consents; God consistently works through willing human agents in Scripture (Abraham, Moses, the prophets); the Eve/Mary typology (Irenaeus, Justin) requires genuine free consent to reverse Eve's free disobedience; honors Mary's agency.
positions.weaknesses: Makes the Incarnation contingent on a human decision — what if Mary had said no? Seems to limit divine sovereignty; God's eternal plan cannot depend on human cooperation; the analogy with Eve is typological, not strictly causal.

positions.label: Divine sovereignty view
positions.proponents: God’s election of Mary was unconditional; her consent is the gracious response to what God was already doing, not a condition of its occurrence.
positions.argument: Election precedes consent
positions.strengths: The Incarnation is God's eternal decree (Gal 4:4: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son'); Mary's response is genuine but enabled by grace — she could not have refused because God's purposes are irresistible; 'I am the Lord's servant' is a statement of submission, not negotiation.
positions.weaknesses: If Mary could not have refused, the dialogue with Gabriel is pro forma — reducing the narrative's drama and Mary's agency; the text presents Mary's response as a decision, not a compelled reaction; may seem to instrumentalize Mary.

synthesis: The debate mirrors the broader sovereignty/free-will question. Catholic Mariology sees Mary's consent as genuinely free and genuinely necessary — God chose to work through willing human cooperation. Reformed theology sees Mary's consent as a response enabled by prevenient grace — she could not have refused because God's purposes are irresistible. Both traditions honor Mary; they differ on the nature of her agency. The text itself presents the exchange as a genuine dialogue (Mary questions, Gabriel explains, Mary consents), which at minimum suggests the narrator values Mary's willing response.

title: Was the census historical?
question: Was there a historical census under Quirinius that brought Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem?
context: Luke 2:1-5 states that Caesar Augustus issued a decree for a census of 'the entire Roman world' and that 'this was the first census that took place while Quirinius was governor of Syria.' The known census under Quirinius occurred in AD 6-7, a decade after Herod's death (4 BC) — the time frame required by Matthew's nativity. This apparent anachronism has been debated for centuries. Defenders of historicity propose an earlier census under Quirinius, a different translation of prōtē ('before Quirinius'), or an administrative arrangement we lack records for.

positions.label: Historical view
positions.proponents: Luke’s census fits within the Augustan census programme (documented by Josephus and papyri). An earlier Quirinius census or a different official is possible.
positions.argument: Historical
positions.strengths: Luke is demonstrably accurate on other historical details (Acts officials, geographical references); Quirinius may have served in an earlier administrative capacity before his known governorship; the decree may have been regional, not empire-wide; papyrus evidence from Egypt shows periodic censuses every 14 years.
positions.weaknesses: No direct evidence of a census under Quirinius before AD 6; the known census (AD 6-7) postdates Herod's death by a decade; requiring people to travel to ancestral cities for a Roman census is historically unusual.

positions.label: Anachronistic view
positions.proponents: Luke may have confused a later AD 6 Quirinius census with Herod’s reign. The historical difficulty is acknowledged even among conservative scholars.
positions.argument: Possible error in attribution
positions.strengths: The known Quirinius census (AD 6-7, recorded by Josephus) does not align with Herod's reign (d. 4 BC); no evidence of an empire-wide census under Augustus; Luke may have combined a theological purpose (connecting Jesus' birth to world events) with imprecise historical detail; ancient historians were less concerned with chronological precision than modern readers expect.
positions.weaknesses: Luke demonstrates remarkable accuracy elsewhere — attributing error here is inconsistent; absence of evidence for an earlier census is not evidence of absence; Luke may have had access to sources Josephus did not.

synthesis: The chronological difficulty is real and has not been definitively resolved. The historical-accuracy proposals are plausible but speculative — no direct evidence confirms a census under Quirinius before AD 6. The anachronism view explains the difficulty but attributes historical error to Luke, whom the same scholars often praise for accuracy elsewhere (Acts itineraries, titles of officials). Most evangelical scholars hold that Luke, generally reliable in historical details, likely had access to information we lack, while acknowledging that the specific mechanism remains unclear.

title: Does Luke’s genealogy represent Mary’s or Joseph’s lineage?
question: Does Luke's genealogy (Luke 3:23-38) trace Jesus's line through Joseph's legal ancestry or through Mary's biological ancestry?
context: Matthew and Luke both provide genealogies but they diverge significantly after David. Matthew traces through Solomon; Luke traces through Nathan. Both claim to trace Joseph's line (Matt 1:16, Luke 3:23). The traditional harmonization says Luke actually traces Mary's line despite naming Joseph, arguing that 'son of Heli' means son-in-law. An alternative view holds that both are Joseph's — one legal, one biological — reflecting levirate marriage complications. The virgin birth accounts in both Gospels make Joseph's biological relationship to Jesus irrelevant, raising the question of why genealogies trace through him at all (answer: legal Davidic succession required male lineage).

positions.label: Joseph’s legal lineage
positions.proponents: Luke traces Joseph’s legal ancestry through Nathan; Matthew traces his biological ancestry through Solomon. Both are true of Joseph simultaneously.
positions.argument: Both genealogies are Joseph’s
positions.strengths: Luke explicitly says 'Jesus... was the son, so it was thought, of Joseph, the son of Heli' — the text names Joseph, not Mary; both genealogies tracing through Joseph is consistent with ancient practice where legal succession passes through the father; the divergence from Matthew could reflect levirate marriage or legal adoption dynamics; avoids importing Mary into a text that doesn't name her.
positions.weaknesses: Requires a hypothetical levirate marriage or adoption scenario that no text records; doesn't explain why two genealogies of the same person (Joseph) diverge so dramatically after David; if both trace Joseph, one presumably tracks legal succession and the other biological descent, but neither text signals this distinction; the solution multiplies unverifiable assumptions.

positions.label: Mary’s biological lineage
positions.proponents: Some propose that Luke’s “Heli” was Mary’s father, making this her lineage transmitted through Joseph’s legal adoption.
positions.argument: Mary’s lineage
positions.strengths: Solves the divergence problem cleanly — Matthew traces Joseph's royal line, Luke traces Mary's biological line; explains why Luke's genealogy appears after the virgin birth narrative (3:23 notes 'as was supposed'); provides Jesus with actual Davidic blood descent through Mary, not just legal succession through Joseph; the Talmud may reference Mary as 'daughter of Heli' (though the identification is disputed).
positions.weaknesses: Luke names Joseph, not Mary — 'son of Heli' would have to mean 'son-in-law of Heli,' which is an unusual and unsupported use; ancient genealogies traced male lines; no early church father before the 5th century clearly articulated the Marian reading; the hypothesis requires reading Mary into a text that does not mention her; assumes Heli had no sons, which is unverifiable.

synthesis: No fully satisfying resolution exists. The Marian reading solves the divergence problem but creates a grammatical one (Luke names Joseph, not Mary). The dual-Joseph reading solves the grammar but requires complex levirate marriage hypotheses with no textual support. Most scholars acknowledge the problem and note that both genealogies serve theological purposes — Matthew shows Jesus as Abraham's and David's heir through the royal line; Luke shows Jesus as Adam's descendant through a non-royal Davidic line, emphasizing universal humanity.

title: Did the Nazareth rejection precede or follow early Galilean ministry?
question: Did the Nazareth rejection (Luke 4:16-30) occur at the beginning of Jesus's ministry as Luke places it, or later as Mark/Matthew suggest — and did Luke relocate it for programmatic theological reasons?
context: Luke places the Nazareth rejection at the very start of Jesus's public ministry, before any other Galilean activity. Mark (6:1-6) and Matthew (13:53-58) place it much later, after extensive Galilean teaching and miracles. The accounts share similarities (hometown rejection, prophet proverb) but differ in significant details. Luke's version is far more elaborate, with Jesus reading from Isaiah 61 and provoking a violent response. The question is whether Luke intentionally repositioned the event to serve as a programmatic introduction to Jesus's ministry — a thesis statement about acceptance, rejection, and Gentile inclusion.

positions.label: Luke’s order original
positions.proponents: Luke is following a topical arrangement, placing the Nazareth scene first as a programmatic statement of the mission.
positions.argument: Programmatic placement
positions.strengths: Takes Luke's chronological placement at face value; Luke claims to have 'carefully investigated everything from the beginning' and to write 'an orderly account' (1:3); the elaborate detail in Luke's version (Isaiah scroll, specific reading, sermon content) suggests firsthand tradition rather than editorial invention; the Capernaum reference (4:23) could refer to events from Jesus's pre-public ministry period.
positions.weaknesses: Luke 4:23 ('what we heard you did in Capernaum') clearly presupposes prior Capernaum ministry that Luke hasn't yet narrated — an internal chronological contradiction if the Nazareth visit is truly first; Mark's placement (after extensive ministry) better fits the context where hometown people say 'isn't this the carpenter?' — they've heard reports; Luke's 'orderly' (kathexēs) may mean thematic order, not strict chronology.

positions.label: Mark/Matthew order original
positions.proponents: Mark 6 and Matthew 13 place the Nazareth rejection after substantial Galilean ministry. Luke has moved it forward for theological reasons.
positions.argument: Topical rearrangement
positions.strengths: Mark and Matthew independently agree on later placement; Luke 4:23's reference to Capernaum ministry is a strong internal signal that prior ministry occurred; Luke's programmatic use of the event (Spirit-anointing, Isaiah fulfillment, Gentile outreach) explains the repositioning; ancient historians regularly arranged material thematically rather than chronologically; Luke's version is far more theologically developed than Mark's, suggesting editorial expansion.
positions.weaknesses: Requires accepting that Luke intentionally altered chronology, which some view as compromising historical reliability; the differences between Luke's and Mark's accounts are substantial enough that they may describe separate visits to Nazareth; assuming editorial relocation presupposes Markan priority, which not all scholars accept; Luke explicitly claims careful chronological investigation (1:3).

synthesis: The majority of scholars favor Luke's theological repositioning of the event, noting that Luke 4:23 ('do here what we heard you did in Capernaum') presupposes prior ministry that Luke hasn't yet narrated — a strong internal signal that the event occurred later chronologically. Luke placed it first because it programmatically introduces his Gospel's key themes: Spirit-anointing, fulfillment of Isaiah, rejection by Israel, and outreach to Gentiles. Whether this constitutes a single event or combines elements of multiple visits remains debated.

title: Is the miraculous catch in Luke 5 the same event as John 21?
question: Are the miraculous catch of fish in Luke 5:1-11 (at Jesus's call of the disciples) and John 21:1-14 (after the resurrection) the same event placed in different contexts, or two distinct events?
context: Both accounts describe a miraculous catch of fish involving Peter and other disciples on the Sea of Galilee after a fruitless night of fishing. In Luke 5, it occasions Peter's call to discipleship; in John 21, it accompanies Peter's post-resurrection restoration. The structural parallels are striking (night failure, Jesus's instruction, overwhelming catch, Peter's response), but the narrative settings differ completely (pre-ministry vs. post-resurrection). The question matters for historical reliability: if one event, then either Luke or John relocated it; if two events, the parallels suggest deliberate literary echoing.

positions.label: Two different events
positions.proponents: Luke 5 occurs at the beginning of Jesus’s ministry; John 21 occurs after the resurrection. Though structurally parallel, they are different events with different theological functions.
positions.argument: Two events
positions.strengths: The settings are fundamentally different — one is a call narrative at ministry's start, the other a restoration scene after the resurrection; Peter's responses differ (Luke: 'depart from me, a sinful man'; John: throws himself into the water to reach Jesus); John 21 includes specific details absent from Luke (153 fish, charcoal fire, breakfast); the literary bookending of Peter's story (miraculous catch at beginning and end) is theologically powerful and intentional.
positions.weaknesses: The structural parallels are so close that independent occurrence seems unlikely — both involve night failure, Jesus's instruction to cast nets, overwhelming catch, and Peter's dramatic personal response; Occam's razor favors one event rather than two strikingly similar ones; the theological bookending argument could explain why one event was placed in two positions rather than why it happened twice.

positions.label: One event relocated
positions.proponents: Some argue John 21 was originally a pre-resurrection calling story that has been relocated. The similarities are too close to be coincidental.
positions.argument: Possible relocation
positions.strengths: Explains the striking structural parallels without positing two near-identical miracles; ancient biographical writing regularly repositioned events for thematic purposes; either Luke moved a post-resurrection event to serve as a call narrative, or John used a pre-ministry tradition as a restoration framework; reduces the number of required historical events.
positions.weaknesses: Requires one evangelist to have significantly relocated a major event — either Luke placed a resurrection appearance before the ministry, or John placed a call narrative after the resurrection; the details that differ (Peter's reaction, fish count, meal) suggest distinct traditions rather than one relocated event; both Gospels integrate the event deeply into their surrounding narratives in ways that resist transplanting.

synthesis: The two-event reading is favored by the majority of scholars who note that the settings are irreconcilably different (call narrative vs. restoration narrative) and that deliberate literary echoing between the beginning and end of Jesus's ministry makes strong theological sense — Peter's story begins and ends with a miraculous catch. The single-event reading struggles to explain how the same event could function in such different narrative contexts. The structural parallels are best explained as intentional literary bookending rather than historical confusion.

title: Are Luke’s beatitudes and Matthew’s from the same sermon?
question: Are Luke's Beatitudes (Luke 6:20-26) and Matthew's (Matthew 5:3-12) two accounts of the same sermon, or records of similar teaching delivered on separate occasions?
context: Matthew's Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) and Luke's Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:17-49) share extensive material but differ in setting (mountain vs. level place), audience (crowds vs. disciples), and content (Matthew has 9 beatitudes with spiritual qualifications — 'poor in spirit'; Luke has 4 beatitudes and 4 woes with material focus — 'you who are poor'). Itinerant teachers routinely repeated core material. The question is whether the Gospel writers drew on one event or two, and whether the differences reflect editorial shaping or distinct occasions.

positions.label: One sermon, two accounts
positions.proponents: Matthew and Luke preserve different aspects of the same teaching event. Matthew has expanded and arranged thematically; Luke has the more original form.
positions.argument: Same sermon
positions.strengths: The overlap is extensive — not just the beatitudes but the core teaching on love of enemies, judging, and the house built on rock; the structural parallels are too close for independent sermons; Matthew's 'poor in spirit' and Luke's 'poor' may interpret the same Aramaic original differently; ancient historians regularly adapted speeches for their audiences while reporting the same event.
positions.weaknesses: The settings differ (mountain vs. level place); Luke has 4 woes that Matthew lacks entirely — if they heard the same sermon, Matthew's omission of woes is a significant editorial decision; Luke's beatitudes are addressed in second person ('blessed are you') while Matthew uses third person ('blessed are the poor in spirit'); the audience composition differs.

positions.label: Two different sermons
positions.proponents: Jesus taught similar material on multiple occasions. Luke and Matthew reflect different teaching events.
positions.argument: Two occasions
positions.strengths: Itinerant teachers repeated core material — Jesus likely preached similar content many times; the different settings (mountain vs. plain) suggest different occasions; Luke's material focus ('poor,' 'hungry,' 'weeping') differs significantly from Matthew's spiritual focus ('poor in spirit,' 'hunger for righteousness'); the woes in Luke have no parallel in Matthew, suggesting different sermons with partially overlapping content; respects both evangelists as accurate reporters of distinct events.
positions.weaknesses: The structural parallels are unusually close for independent sermons — both end with the house-on-rock parable in nearly identical form; requires Jesus to have delivered strikingly similar sermons with the same structure, examples, and conclusion; Occam's razor favors one event adapted by two writers over two near-identical events; the two-sermon view multiplies entities beyond necessity.

synthesis: Both positions have strong supporters. The one-sermon view emphasizes the extensive overlap and argues the differences reflect each evangelist's theological emphasis. The two-sermon view emphasizes the different settings and the substantive differences in content. The question may be unanswerable with certainty. What scholars agree on is that both versions authentically represent Jesus's teaching, that itinerant rabbis repeated core themes, and that the evangelists shaped their material for their audiences.

title: Is Luke 7's anointing the same as John 12?
question: Is the anointing by a sinful woman in Luke 7:36-50 the same event as Mary of Bethany's anointing in John 12:1-8, or are these distinct incidents?
context: Both accounts describe a woman anointing Jesus during a meal at a man named Simon's house. The parallels are obvious; the differences are significant. In Luke, the woman is unnamed, described as 'a sinner,' and anoints Jesus's feet with tears and perfume; the host is 'Simon the Pharisee'; and Jesus tells a parable about forgiveness. In John, the woman is Mary of Bethany (Lazarus's sister), the setting is Bethany near Jerusalem, and the objection comes from Judas about wasted money. Mark 14:3-9 and Matthew 26:6-13 describe yet another anointing at 'Simon the Leper's' house. The tradition that conflated these women into 'Mary Magdalene' has no textual basis.

positions.label: Different events
positions.proponents: Luke's anointing is earlier, by a sinful woman; John 12's is by Mary of Bethany before the Passion. The theological purposes are different.
positions.argument: Two events
positions.strengths: The women are different — Luke's is unnamed and sinful; John identifies Mary of Bethany, a respected disciple; the settings differ (Galilee in Luke, Bethany in John); the hosts differ (Simon the Pharisee vs. Lazarus's household, though Mark has 'Simon the Leper'); the theological points differ entirely (forgiveness in Luke, burial preparation in John); Luke's placement is mid-ministry, John's is passion week; the early church distinguished the women before Gregory's conflation.
positions.weaknesses: The coincidence of shared elements (anointing, dinner, Simon, objection) is striking for independent events; the Fourth Gospel may have access to the same tradition Luke used and retold it in a Bethany context; Jesus was anointed at meals often enough to be remarkable, which itself suggests these may be one event retold differently.

positions.label: Same event, relocated
positions.proponents: The structural parallels are close enough that some argue Luke has relocated the Bethany anointing.
positions.argument: One relocated event
positions.strengths: The shared elements (woman, anointing, meal, host named Simon, objection) constitute a strong pattern; ancient narrators routinely adapted a single tradition for different narrative purposes; the differences may reflect theological shaping rather than historical distinctness; John 11:2 identifies Mary as 'the one who anointed the Lord,' which may reference a well-known single event tradition.
positions.weaknesses: Requires Luke or John to have substantially altered the woman's identity, setting, and theological significance — a level of editorial freedom that strains historical credibility; Luke's 'sinful woman' and John's 'Mary of Bethany' are not reconcilable without special pleading; the narrative functions are so different (forgiveness vs. burial) that treating them as one event requires one evangelist to have fundamentally misunderstood the occasion; Gregory the Great's conflation is now rejected by Catholic and Protestant scholars alike.

synthesis: Most scholars treat Luke 7 and John 12 as distinct events, noting the irreconcilable differences in the women's identities, the host's characterization, the narrative function, and the theological point. Luke's account is about forgiveness and gratitude; John's is about extravagant devotion anticipating burial. The shared elements (anointing, Simon, meal) likely reflect the commonality of such social settings rather than a single event reported differently. The conflation of these women in Western tradition (Gregory the Great, 591 AD) is now widely rejected.

title: Did the women travel with Jesus full-time or provide financial support from their homes?
question: Did the women mentioned in Luke 8:1-3 travel with Jesus's itinerant group full-time, or did they provide financial support from their homes while remaining in their communities?
context: Luke 8:1-3 uniquely mentions women who accompanied Jesus: Mary Magdalene, Joanna (wife of Chuza, Herod's steward), Susanna, and 'many others.' These women 'were helping to support them out of their own means.' The text says they traveled 'with him' (syn autō), which grammatically suggests physical accompaniment. However, in a first-century Palestinian context, women traveling with an unrelated male teacher was socially scandalous. The question intersects with debates about women's roles in early Christianity and the social dynamics of Jesus's movement.

positions.label: Full-time travel
positions.proponents: 8:1-3 implies they traveled with the Twelve — "from one town and village to another... The Twelve were with him, and also some women."
positions.argument: Itinerant
positions.strengths: Luke says they went 'with him' (syn autō) as he traveled through towns and villages — the grammar describes physical accompaniment; Mark 15:41 confirms that women 'followed him' (ēkolouthoun) and 'served him' (diēkonoun) in Galilee, using discipleship language; their presence at the cross and tomb (Luke 23:49, 55) presupposes they traveled with Jesus to Jerusalem; the verb 'accompany' (synoδeúō) in the broader passage implies physical presence, not remote support.
positions.weaknesses: Full-time travel by women with an unrelated male group would have been deeply scandalous in first-century Palestine; Joanna was wife of Herod's steward — her absence from home would have required extraordinary social permission; the economic logistics of supporting an itinerant group 'out of their own means' may favor home-based wealth management over personal travel; Luke may be telescoping periodic accompaniment into a summary statement.

positions.label: Financial supporters from home
positions.proponents: The women provided resources from their own means while primarily based at home, not necessarily on the full traveling circuit.
positions.argument: Home-based
positions.strengths: The phrase 'helping to support them out of their own means' (diēkonoun autois ek tōn hyparchontōn autais) emphasizes financial provision, which could be managed remotely; first-century Jewish social norms made full-time mixed-gender travel nearly impossible for married women like Joanna; wealthy patronage of religious teachers was an established practice that did not require physical presence; preserves social plausibility within the historical context.
positions.weaknesses: The text explicitly says the women traveled 'with him' — the home-based reading must override or soften clear language; Mark 15:41 uses 'followed' (akoloutheō), the standard term for discipleship through physical accompaniment; the women's presence at crucifixion and burial in all four Gospels requires their presence in Jerusalem, implying they did travel; the reading may be motivated by discomfort with the social implications rather than by the text's plain meaning.

synthesis: The text's language ('with him,' traveling from 'one town and village to another') most naturally supports the full-time travel reading. Luke's specificity in naming these women and their financial role suggests a deliberate, historically grounded tradition. The social scandal of mixed-gender itinerancy was likely real and intentional — consistent with Jesus's pattern of transgressing social boundaries. Most scholars accept that at least some women traveled with the group, though the exact extent and consistency of their presence cannot be determined from the brief notice.

title: What does 9:27 mean: "some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God"?
question: What does 9:27 mean: "some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God"?

positions.label: The Transfiguration
positions.proponents: The most natural referent: the Transfiguration follows immediately (9:28) and is a proleptic vision of the kingdom’s glory.
positions.argument: Transfiguration

positions.label: Pentecost and the church
positions.proponents: The kingdom’s visible arrival in the community of the Spirit, experienced by those who survived to Pentecost.
positions.argument: Pentecost

title: Is the Good Samaritan an allegory?
question: Is the Good Samaritan an allegory?

positions.label: Pure parable
positions.proponents: The parable answers the question of who is a neighbor. The allegorical interpretation (Adam = mankind, Samaritan = Christ, etc.) is a secondary patristic imposition on the text.
positions.argument: Historical-literal

positions.label: Christological allegory
positions.proponents: Origen’s reading has the Samaritan as Christ is consistent with Luke’s Christology and the language of compassion used for Jesus throughout.
positions.argument: Christological

title: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?
question: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?

positions.label: Luke's emphasis: the preacher
positions.argument: Luke's version focuses on Jonah as a preacher — his whole mission to Nineveh is the sign. The Son of Man's preached word will be the sign to this generation.

positions.label: Matthew's emphasis: the resurrection
positions.argument: Matthew (12:40) specifies the three days in the fish as a type of the resurrection. The sign is death and vindication.

title: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?
question: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?

positions.label: Luke preserves the original
positions.argument: Luke's shorter form is likely closer to the original. Matthew expanded it for liturgical use — the additions are consistent with his other expansions of Q material.

positions.label: Matthew preserves the original
positions.argument: Matthew's fuller form may reflect a carefully transmitted liturgical tradition from the Jerusalem community.

title: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?
question: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?

positions.label: Fire of the Spirit
positions.argument: The fire Jesus came to cast (v.49) is the fire of the Holy Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2:3-4). The "baptism" of v.50 and the Spirit's fire are two sides of the same event.

positions.label: Fire of judgment and division
positions.argument: The fire is the eschatological judgment that Jesus's arrival initiates — consistent with John the Baptist's fire-and-Spirit imagery (3:16-17) and the division language of vv.51-53.

title: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?
question: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?

positions.label: Yes, graduated eschatological punishment
positions.argument: The passage implies that punishment in the final judgment is proportionate to knowledge and responsibility — a teaching consistent with Rom 2:12-16 and Heb 10:29.

positions.label: Contextual hyperbole about earthly discipline
positions.argument: The punishment language refers to the consequences of unfaithfulness in this life and in the community, not necessarily to eternal states.

title: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?
question: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?

positions.label: Palm Sunday
positions.argument: The immediate narrative context supports a near-term reference: 19:38 records the crowd's "Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord!" as Jesus enters Jerusalem. The saying is fulfilled within the same travel narrative.

positions.label: The Parousia
positions.argument: The full recognition implied — Jerusalem "seeing" Jesus with the Hallel acclamation — was not achieved at the Palm Sunday entry, where Jesus was rejected within days. The ultimate fulfilment is eschatological.

title: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?
question: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?

positions.label: Three-stage allegorical reading
positions.argument: The three invitation stages correspond to: (1) the religious establishment's refusal; (2) inclusion of tax collectors and sinners within Israel; (3) the Gentile mission. The parable encodes the history of the early church's mission.

positions.label: Social criticism without allegory
positions.argument: The parable is primarily a subversive social critique of elite reciprocity culture, not an allegory of salvation history. The allegorical reading domesticates what is a more radical social challenge.

title: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?
question: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?

positions.label: Elder son condemned
positions.argument: The elder's resentment represents a fundamental failure of love that the parable exposes as disqualifying. He is the Pharisees; his exclusion from the feast is his own choice and its consequences are permanent.

positions.label: Elder son invited, not condemned
positions.argument: The parable does not end with the elder son refusing. The father goes out and pleads — as he went out to the younger. The open ending is an invitation: the Pharisees can still enter. The parable is evangelistic toward the elder son, not merely critical.

title: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?
question: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?

positions.label: Literal afterlife geography
positions.argument: The parable describes actual conditions in the intermediate state: conscious existence, recognition, memory, the chasm, and the finality of post-death conditions. Jesus is teaching doctrine through narrative.

positions.label: Narrative illustration using conventional imagery
positions.argument: Jesus uses the conventional Jewish imagery of Hades (from 1 Enoch, rabbinic tradition) to illustrate his point about wealth and poverty. The point is the reversal and the sufficiency of Scripture, not a map of the afterlife.

title: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?
question: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?

positions.label: Within you (interior)
positions.argument: Entos normally means "within/inside." The kingdom is an interior spiritual reality present in the hearts of those who receive it. This reading shaped centuries of Christian spirituality.

positions.label: In your midst (among you)
positions.proponents: Most modern scholars: Fitzmyer, Green, Marshall, Bock
positions.argument: Jesus would not say the kingdom is "within" the Pharisees who oppose him. Luke consistently presents the kingdom as present in Jesus's ministry and person. Entos can mean "among" (Ps 109:30 LXX uses it this way).

title: Why does Jesus ask "Why do you call me good?" (18:19)?
question: Is Jesus denying his own goodness, affirming his deity, or correcting flattery?
context: When a ruler addresses Jesus as 'Good Teacher,' Jesus responds: 'Why do you call me good? No one is good — except God alone' (Luke 18:19; cf. Mark 10:17-18). Skeptics have cited this as Jesus denying his own divinity. Orthodox interpreters see it as an implicit claim to deity — if only God is good and Jesus is good, then Jesus is God. Others see Jesus simply redirecting the ruler's superficial compliment toward the substance of his question about eternal life.

positions.label: Jesus denies his own goodness
positions.argument: Jesus is deflecting the title "good teacher" because he does not consider himself divine. This reading uses the saying to argue against early high Christology.
positions.strengths: The surface reading is a denial — 'Don't call me good; only God is good'; if Jesus is not God, this is a straightforward correction; consistent with a purely human Jesus who defers to God's unique goodness.
positions.weaknesses: Contradicts the Gospels' own claims about Jesus (John 10:11, 'the good shepherd'; the sinlessness claim in Heb 4:15); the early church would not have preserved a verse that denied Jesus' deity/goodness if that were its meaning; ignores the pedagogical context.

positions.label: Jesus is testing the ruler's theology
positions.argument: Jesus is not denying his own goodness but pressing the ruler: do you understand what you're saying? "Good" belongs to God alone — so who do you think I am? The question is an invitation to Christological recognition, not a denial of divinity.
positions.strengths: Jesus' question is diagnostic: 'Do you know what you're saying?' If only God is good, and you call me good, what does that imply about who I am? This is Jesus' typical indirect method of self-revelation; the ruler used 'good' as flattery without considering its theological implications.
positions.weaknesses: The implicit deity claim is so subtle that the ruler (and many readers) miss it; if Jesus wanted to claim deity, he could have been more direct; the interpretation requires reading between the lines.

positions.label: Jesus is correcting flattery
positions.argument: The ruler is using "Good teacher" as a social honorific. Jesus redirects: goodness is a divine attribute, not a teaching compliment. The saying is about theological seriousness, not Christology.
positions.strengths: The ruler uses 'Good Teacher' as a respectful but superficial address; Jesus redirects from compliment to content — 'stop flattering me and engage with the commandments'; the parallel in Matthew 19:17 ('Why do you ask me about what is good?') softens the christological edge; the point is pedagogical, not theological.
positions.weaknesses: Reduces a theologically rich statement to mere social correction; Jesus' invocation of God's unique goodness is too weighty for simple anti-flattery; misses the christological implications that the wider Gospel context supports.

synthesis: The verse is a masterful pedagogical response rather than a theological self-disclosure. Jesus is not denying his goodness (which would contradict the Gospels' broader Christology) or making an explicit deity claim (which would be out of character for his indirect self-revelation). He is redirecting the ruler's attention: 'Do you realize what you are saying by calling me good? Only God is good. Are you prepared to follow that logic?' The question is diagnostic, not definitional.

title: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?
question: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?

positions.label: A promise: present conversion
positions.argument: Zacchaeus's present tense ("I give" / "I will pay back") represents a declaration of immediate intent — his spontaneous response to Jesus's presence. This reading makes the story a conversion narrative.

positions.label: A defence: describing current practice
positions.argument: Zacchaeus is defending himself against the crowd's accusation: "Look, I already do give half... and when I have defrauded someone, I already do repay fourfold." On this reading, the story is about unjust accusation, not conversion.

title: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?
question: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?

positions.label: Coin/tax only
positions.argument: Jesus is making a practical point about Roman taxation: coins bearing Caesar's image belong in Caesar's domain; taxes are therefore legitimate. The God-claim is about Temple tithes and religious obligations.

positions.label: Humans as God's image
positions.argument: Since humans bear God's image (Gen 1:26), the full answer to "give to God what is God's" is: give yourself — totally — to God. The eikōn wordplay is intentional and subversive: Caesar gets his coins; God gets everything that bears his image, which is you.

title: "This generation" in 21:32: Near or Far Fulfilment?
question: Does 'this generation will not pass away' (Luke 21:32) refer to the AD 70 destruction, the end times, or the Jewish people as a whole?
context: In the Olivet Discourse, Jesus describes signs of the end and then declares: 'Truly I tell you, this generation will not pass away until all these things have happened' (Luke 21:32). The difficulty is that 'all these things' seems to include both the destruction of Jerusalem (which happened in AD 70) and cosmic-eschatological events (Son of Man coming in glory, 21:27). If 'this generation' means Jesus' contemporaries, then either everything was fulfilled in AD 70 (preterism) or Jesus was wrong about the timing (a position most Christians reject). The verse is central to debates about eschatology.

positions.label: Entirely fulfilled in AD 70
positions.proponents: Preterist interpreters: N.T. Wright, R.C. Sproul
positions.argument: "This generation" refers to the generation alive in AD 30; "all these things" refers to the Jerusalem destruction of AD 70. Luke 21:20-24 describes the siege; the Parousia language (21:25-27) is cosmic imagery for a historical event.
positions.strengths: The destruction of Jerusalem occurred within one generation (c. 40 years); Luke's version of the discourse focuses more on the fall of Jerusalem than Matthew or Mark; the cosmic language (sun darkened, stars falling) is standard OT judgment imagery (Isa 13:10; Joel 2:31), not literal astronomical events.
positions.weaknesses: The 'Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory' (21:27) was not visibly fulfilled in AD 70; full preterism requires the Second Coming to have already occurred, which contradicts the church's consistent expectation of a future return; the 'redemption drawing near' (21:28) was not experienced by the AD 70 generation.

positions.label: Partially fulfilled in AD 70, fully at Parousia
positions.argument: The near events (AD 70) and far events (Parousia) are both in view; "this generation" may extend beyond a single human lifetime. A double fulfilment, with AD 70 as a type of the final end.
positions.strengths: Prophetic discourse characteristically blends near and far horizons (cf. Isa 7:14 — immediate and messianic fulfillments); the fall of Jerusalem is a 'type' of the final judgment; 'this generation' refers to Jesus' contemporaries who will see the beginning (AD 70) of what culminates at the Parousia.
positions.weaknesses: 'All these things' is comprehensive — partial fulfillment seems to dodge the text's force; the blended-horizon approach can become an interpretive escape hatch for any difficult prophecy; the audience would have heard 'this generation' as their generation.

positions.label: "Generation" = the Jewish people
positions.argument: "This generation" (genea) means the Jewish people as an enduring race — they will persist until the final eschatological fulfilment.
positions.strengths: genea can mean 'kind' or 'race' (cf. Ps 12:7 LXX; Phil 2:15); the promise becomes: 'the Jewish people will not pass away until all is fulfilled' — a remarkable and verifiable statement; avoids the timing problem entirely.
positions.weaknesses: The primary meaning of genea is 'generation' (contemporaries), not 'race'; this reading is motivated by theological difficulty rather than lexical evidence; the audience would not have heard 'race' when Jesus said genea in this context.

synthesis: The debate remains unresolved. The AD 70 reading works well for Luke 21:20-24 (siege of Jerusalem) but struggles with 21:25-28 (cosmic signs, Son of Man coming). The 'generation' = 'race' reading avoids the timing problem but stretches the normal meaning of genea. The partial-fulfillment reading acknowledges that prophetic discourse characteristically blends near and far horizons — a feature of OT prophecy as well (Isaiah's Servant Songs, Daniel's kingdoms). Most scholars agree that Jesus' language deliberately maintains an eschatological tension between 'soon' and 'not yet.'

title: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?
question: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: The passage is present in many important early witnesses and absent mainly in Alexandrian manuscripts. Its omission may reflect early theological embarrassment about Christ's agony and its implications for his divinity. The stylistic features are consistent with Luke.

positions.label: Later addition
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, the corrector of Sinaiticus) omit it. Early Church Fathers sometimes seem unaware of it. The theological difficulty it presents may explain its original absence rather than subsequent omission.

title: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?
question: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: Present in Sinaiticus, A, C, D and many others. Absent in p75, B, W and a few others. Internal evidence strongly favours inclusion: stylistic consistency with Luke, the prayer for persecutors echoed in Acts 7:60 (Stephen), and the obvious motive for early omission (anti-Jewish sentiment).

positions.label: Later addition
positions.proponents: Some scholars, Westcott-Hort originally excluded it
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, B) lack it. The motive for addition is clear (theological enhancement). The silence of some early Fathers may indicate non-awareness.

title: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?
question: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?

positions.label: Yes, eucharistic allusion is deliberate
positions.proponents: Fitzmyer, Green, most Catholic and many Protestant scholars
positions.argument: The sequence — took bread, gave thanks, broke it, gave it (24:30) — exactly parallels the Last Supper (22:19) and the feeding of the five thousand (9:16). Luke uses this four-verb pattern consistently for the Eucharist. The recognition at the breaking of bread is a model for Eucharistic presence of the risen Christ.

positions.label: Ordinary hospitality, not eucharistic
positions.argument: The disciples are guests at Emmaus, not officiants at a sacrament. The four-verb pattern is simply how meals are described. The theological point is about hospitality as the occasion of recognition, not about a specific sacramental theology.

title: Is the Prologue a Pre-existing Hymn?
question: Is John's Prologue (1:1-18) an adaptation of a pre-existing hymn, or an original composition by the evangelist?
context: John 1:1-18 is among the most theologically dense passages in the NT. Its poetic structure, elevated language, and Logos theology have led many scholars to propose that it was originally an independent hymn — perhaps from a Johannine community or adapted from a Jewish Wisdom hymn — that the evangelist incorporated and edited (adding the prose insertions about John the Baptist, vv. 6-8, 15). The alternative view holds that the Prologue was composed by the evangelist as an introduction, its poetic quality reflecting deliberate theological rhetoric.

positions.label: Yes: hymnic adaptation
positions.argument: The prologue has a poetic, rhythmic structure distinct from the surrounding narrative, with prose insertions about John the Baptist (vv.6-8, 15). The original hymn may have been a Johannine community hymn to the Logos that the evangelist adapted.
positions.strengths: The poetic structure is recognizable and can be reconstructed by removing prose insertions (vv. 6-8, 15); other NT christological passages are widely considered pre-existing hymns (Phil 2:6-11; Col 1:15-20); the Logos theology draws on Jewish Wisdom traditions that predate the Gospel; the Baptist insertions disrupt the poem's flow, suggesting editorial addition.
positions.weaknesses: The reconstructed hymn varies by scholar — no consensus exists on its exact form; the vocabulary is thoroughly Johannine, making an external origin less certain; the 'insertions' could be intentional narrative asides rather than editorial additions.

positions.label: No: unified composition
positions.argument: The supposed hymnic structure is not consistent enough to identify a discrete pre-existing hymn. The prologue is the evangelist's own introduction, written in elevated prose that reflects the content's grandeur.
positions.strengths: The vocabulary and theology are continuous with the rest of the Gospel; the evangelist was capable of writing elevated theological prose; the 'insertions' about the Baptist serve the Gospel's polemic against Baptist sect claims; the poetic quality reflects deliberate rhetorical crafting, not necessarily a pre-existing source.
positions.weaknesses: The structural parallels with other NT hymns (Phil 2; Col 1) suggest a recognized hymnic genre; the tonal shift between the poetic sections and the Baptist prose is difficult to explain as unified composition; the Wisdom parallels suggest the evangelist drew on an existing tradition.

synthesis: The hymnic hypothesis has strong support — the rhythmic structure, the Baptist insertions, and the parallel with Philippians 2:6-11 and Colossians 1:15-20 (other NT passages widely considered pre-existing hymns). However, the vocabulary and theology of the Prologue are deeply Johannine, suggesting at minimum heavy editorial shaping. Whether the evangelist adapted an existing hymn or composed an original poetic introduction, the final form is fully integrated into the Gospel's theology.

title: One Temple Cleansing or Two?
question: Did Jesus cleanse the temple once (with John or the Synoptics relocating it) or twice (once at the start and once at the end of his ministry)?
context: John places the temple cleansing at the beginning of Jesus' ministry (John 2:13-22), while the Synoptics place it during the final week (Matt 21:12-13; Mark 11:15-17; Luke 19:45-46). The two-cleansing theory holds that Jesus drove out money-changers twice — once early and once late. The one-cleansing theory holds that there was a single event which John relocated for theological reasons (programmatic opening) or which the Synoptics placed at the end as the trigger for the arrest. The details differ significantly between John and the Synoptics.

positions.label: Two cleansings: one at start, one at end
positions.argument: The differences between John and the Synoptics (timing, details, context) are best explained by two separate events. Jesus's prophetic action would naturally provoke escalating hostility over three years.
positions.strengths: Takes both John and the Synoptics at face value without requiring relocation; the details differ (John mentions a whip, quotes Psalm 69; Synoptics quote Isaiah 56 and Jeremiah 7); Jesus' prophetic authority could warrant two confrontations; the traditional harmonization preserves historical accuracy of all four Gospels.
positions.weaknesses: It is implausible that temple authorities would permit a second cleansing after the first; the Synoptics show no awareness of an earlier cleansing; the two-event theory multiplies entities beyond what the evidence requires.

positions.label: One cleansing: John moved it for theological reasons
positions.argument: John deliberately placed the cleansing at the beginning to introduce the temple-body theme early. Historical-critical scholarship generally prefers the Synoptic timing as more historically plausible and sees John's placement as theological redaction.
positions.strengths: The simplest explanation — one event, two placements; John regularly arranges material thematically rather than chronologically; the temple cleansing at the Gospel's opening programmatically establishes Jesus as the replacement of the temple (2:19-21); the Synoptic placement as the trigger for arrest makes historical sense.
positions.weaknesses: Requires John to have deliberately moved a historical event, raising questions about historical reliability; John's Gospel claims eyewitness testimony (21:24); some readers are uncomfortable with the implication that any Gospel rearranges chronology.

synthesis: The two-cleansing view is the traditional harmonization but struggles with the implausibility of the temple authorities permitting a second disruption. The one-cleansing view is simpler and widely held, but requires either John or the Synoptics to have relocated the event. Most scholars favor a single cleansing, noting that John frequently rearranges chronology for theological purposes (e.g., the Last Supper timing). The event's placement in each Gospel serves the respective narrative theology — in John it reveals Jesus as the new temple; in the Synoptics it precipitates the passion.

title: "Water and Spirit" in 3:5: Baptism or Regeneration?
question: When Jesus says one must be 'born of water and the Spirit' (John 3:5), does 'water' refer to baptism, the Spirit's cleansing, or physical birth?
context: In Jesus' conversation with Nicodemus, he declares: 'No one can enter the kingdom of God unless they are born of water and the Spirit' (John 3:5). The meaning of 'water' has divided interpreters since the patristic era. Sacramental traditions (Catholic, Orthodox, Lutheran) identify water with baptism. Reformed and evangelical interpreters variously identify it with the Spirit's cleansing work (hendiadys: 'water, even the Spirit'), physical birth (amniotic fluid), or the Word of God. The interpretation affects baptismal theology and the necessity of sacraments for salvation.

positions.label: Water = baptism
positions.proponents: Catholic and many patristic interpreters, Luther
positions.argument: The natural reading of "water" in a baptism context (John 3 follows John's baptising ministry) is Christian baptism. The new birth is sacramentally enacted in baptism. Augustine, Chrysostom, and Luther all hold this view.
positions.strengths: The immediate context involves John's baptism (1:26-33) and Jesus' own baptizing ministry (3:22); Ezekiel 36:25-27 combines water sprinkling with spiritual renewal; early church unanimously connected this verse to baptism; Titus 3:5 links washing/regeneration.
positions.weaknesses: Making baptism necessary for salvation raises difficult questions about the thief on the cross and unbaptized believers; John's Gospel elsewhere emphasizes faith, not sacraments, as the means of receiving life (3:16, 5:24, 6:47).

positions.label: Water = the Spirit's cleansing work (hendiadys)
positions.argument: Water and Spirit describe the same event — the Spirit's purifying regenerative work promised in Ezek 36:25-27. The OT background and the immediate context (Nicodemus doesn't know baptism yet) favour this reading.
positions.strengths: 'Water and Spirit' may be hendiadys — one concept expressed with two words: 'water-Spirit' = the Spirit's cleansing work; Ezekiel 36:25-27 uses water imagery for spiritual purification; avoids making a physical rite essential for salvation; consistent with John's emphasis on faith (3:16).
positions.weaknesses: Hendiadys is possible but not the most natural reading of kai ('and'); the early church did not read it this way; requires a specialized grammatical argument.

positions.label: Water = physical birth
positions.argument: "Born of water" = amniotic fluid (natural birth); "born of Spirit" = spiritual birth. Jesus is distinguishing natural from spiritual birth.
positions.strengths: Creates a clear parallelism: born of water (physical) and Spirit (spiritual); Nicodemus's confusion about 'entering the womb' (3:4) sets up the physical-spiritual contrast; Jesus distinguishes 'flesh' from 'Spirit' in 3:6.
positions.weaknesses: 'Water' is not used for amniotic fluid or physical birth in any Jewish text; the flesh/Spirit distinction in 3:6 is Jesus' explanation of 3:5, not a restatement of water/Spirit; the physical-birth reading is a modern proposal with no ancient support.

synthesis: The baptismal reading has the strongest patristic support and fits the immediate context (John's water baptism, 1:26-33; 3:22-23). The hendiadys reading avoids making baptism essential for salvation. The physical-birth reading creates a neat parallelism (physical birth + spiritual birth) but lacks lexical support — 'water' is not used for amniotic fluid in Jewish literature. Most scholars favor either the baptismal or hendiadys reading, with the recognition that John's Gospel regularly uses physical symbols (water, bread, light) with spiritual double meanings.

title: Is the Samaritan Woman a Historical Figure or a Symbol?
question: Is the Samaritan woman at the well a historical person, a symbolic representative, or both?
context: John 4:1-42 presents Jesus' encounter with a Samaritan woman at Jacob's well. The narrative is rich in symbolism: the well (cf. OT betrothal scenes at wells), the 'five husbands' (the five foreign gods of 2 Kings 17:24-33?), the worship question (Gerizim vs. Jerusalem), and the woman's role as the first Samaritan evangelist. The symbolic density has led some to read her as a representative of Samaria rather than a historical individual. The 'five husbands' have been read as the five pagan deities imposed on Samaria after the Assyrian conquest.

positions.label: Historical and symbolic
positions.argument: The encounter is historical (specific geographical details, personal details about the woman's life) but also carries symbolic resonance (five husbands = five foreign nations of 2 Kgs 17; the woman = Samaria as bride). Both levels operate simultaneously.
positions.strengths: John's narrative art operates on multiple levels simultaneously; the woman's personality (her wit, evasion, eventual testimony) is too vivid for a pure symbol; the geographical and cultural details are accurate; the symbolic resonance (well, husbands, worship) enriches a historical encounter without replacing it.
positions.weaknesses: The 'both' reading can seem evasive; if the husbands are symbolic, the historical basis may be thinner than assumed; maintaining both levels requires sophisticated reading that may exceed the narrator's intent.

positions.label: Primarily symbolic
positions.argument: The woman represents Samaria seeking its true husband (God); the five husbands are the five gods imported by the five nations. The personal details are allegorical rather than biographical.
positions.strengths: The five husbands correspond to the five foreign nations (and their gods) imported by Assyria (2 Kgs 17:24-33); the well scene echoes OT betrothal type-scenes (Gen 24; Gen 29; Exod 2); the worship question (Gerizim vs. Jerusalem) is the central Samaritan-Jewish theological divide; the narrative is a theological portrait of Samaria's encounter with the Messiah.
positions.weaknesses: 2 Kings 17 actually lists seven nations, not five; the woman's vivid personality argues against a mere symbol; reducing her to allegory diminishes the narrative's power; the five-husbands-as-gods reading is creative but forced.

synthesis: The most satisfying reading is 'both' — John presents historical encounters with rich symbolic layering. The Samaritan woman is a real person whose specific situation (five husbands, living with a man outside marriage) carries additional resonance as a portrait of Samaria's religious history. John's Gospel consistently operates on multiple levels — literal and symbolic readings are not mutually exclusive but mutually enriching. The woman's individuality (her wit, her questions, her evangelistic testimony) argues against a purely symbolic reading.

title: Realised vs. Future Eschatology in John 5
question: Does John's Gospel teach primarily realized eschatology (salvation is now) or does it also affirm future bodily resurrection?
context: John 5:24-29 contains a striking juxtaposition: 'whoever hears my word and believes has eternal life and has crossed over from death to life' (5:24 — realized eschatology) alongside 'a time is coming when all who are in their graves will hear his voice and come out' (5:28-29 — future eschatology). Bultmann famously argued that the future-eschatology passages were later additions by an ecclesiastical redactor who corrected John's pure realized eschatology. Most scholars now reject this but the tension between present and future in John remains significant.

positions.label: Primarily realised
positions.argument: The emphasis in John is on present possession: "has eternal life," "has crossed over from death to life" (v.24). Future resurrection language (vv.28-29) may be a later ecclesiastical addition to John's original text.
positions.strengths: John's distinctive emphasis is present salvation — 'has eternal life' (5:24), 'has crossed over from death to life' (5:24), 'I am the resurrection and the life' (11:25); the judgment is now (3:18-19; 12:31); Bultmann rightly identified this emphasis as John's unique contribution; the believer's experience of salvation begins at the moment of faith.
positions.weaknesses: John 5:28-29, 6:39-40, 6:44, and 6:54 explicitly affirm future bodily resurrection; Bultmann's redaction theory is rejected by most scholars; stripping the future dimension from John's eschatology distorts it; the 'last day' references are integral to the Gospel's theology.

positions.label: Both present and future, complementary
positions.argument: Both dimensions are authentically Johannine and authentically dominical. Present realisation of eternal life (v.24-25) and future bodily resurrection (v.28-29) are both affirmed; they describe different aspects of the same salvation. The Gospel maintains this both/and throughout.
positions.strengths: John 5:24-29 holds both dimensions together within a single discourse; 'the hour is coming and now is' (5:25) explicitly combines present and future; the 'last day' references (6:39-40, 44, 54) are too numerous to be editorial additions; the two eschatologies are complementary, not contradictory — present participation anticipates future consummation.
positions.weaknesses: The tension between the two eschatologies remains: if the believer 'has' eternal life now (5:24), what does the future resurrection add? The relationship between present experience and future hope is stated but not fully explained; some scholars find the juxtaposition awkward.

synthesis: John holds both dimensions together. Eternal life begins now through faith (5:24; 11:25-26) and will be consummated at the future resurrection (5:28-29; 6:39-40). This is not a contradiction but a both/and: the believer already participates in the eschatological reality that will be fully revealed at the last day. John's distinctive contribution is the emphasis on present realization — a theological complement to, not replacement of, the future hope shared with the Synoptics and Paul.

title: Is John 6:51-58 Eucharistic?
question: Does Jesus' language about eating his flesh and drinking his blood in John 6 refer to the Eucharist or to faith?
context: In the Bread of Life discourse, Jesus declares: 'Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). Sacramental traditions read this as institution of or reference to the Eucharist. Non-sacramental traditions read it as a vivid metaphor for believing in Christ — 'eating' = 'coming to' and 'believing in' Jesus (cf. 6:35). Notably, John's Gospel does not narrate the institution of the Lord's Supper at the Last Supper, which some see as supporting a eucharistic reading here and others as arguing against one.

positions.label: Yes: reference to the Eucharist
positions.proponents: Catholic, Orthodox, many Protestant scholars (Brown, Hoskyns)
positions.argument: The language shifts to the physical trōgō; the flesh/blood pairing is the eucharistic formula; the synagogue setting doesn't preclude post-resurrection sacramental reference. John deliberately replaces the Last Supper institution with this discourse.
positions.strengths: The language becomes increasingly physical — trōgō ('chew/munch,' 6:54) is visceral, not metaphorical; John omits the Last Supper institution narrative, possibly because he placed the eucharistic teaching here; patristic consensus strongly favors this reading.
positions.weaknesses: Jesus explains his words spiritually in 6:63 ('the flesh counts for nothing'); the discourse precedes the Last Supper chronologically; if eucharistic, it makes the sacrament essential for salvation, which tensions with John's faith emphasis.

positions.label: No: metaphorical reference to faith
positions.argument: Verse 63 ("the flesh counts for nothing; the Spirit gives life") interprets the eating as spiritual. The discourse is about faith (v.29, 35, 40, 47) throughout; the "flesh and blood" intensification is a figure for complete appropriation of Christ.
positions.strengths: Jesus explicitly interprets his words spiritually (6:63); 'eating' parallels 'coming' and 'believing' (6:35); the discourse's context is belief, not sacrament; the crowd's misunderstanding is about taking literal what Jesus means spiritually — exactly John's literary technique throughout.
positions.weaknesses: The shift from phagō to trōgō (raw physical eating) is hard to explain if purely metaphorical; ignores the eucharistic overtones that the early church heard in these words; the bread/flesh/blood language is more specific than a general faith metaphor.

positions.label: Both: the discourse is about faith, sacramentally enacted in the Eucharist
positions.argument: The discourse addresses faith in Christ; the Eucharist is the visible enactment of that faith. The sacrament is the appointed means of the spiritual eating the discourse describes.
positions.strengths: John characteristically operates on multiple levels — water is both physical and spiritual (ch. 3-4), sight is both physical and spiritual (ch. 9); the discourse is primarily about faith, which the Eucharist later embodies sacramentally; honors both the spiritual explanation (6:63) and the physical language.
positions.weaknesses: The 'both' reading can seem like a convenient harmonization; if faith is the primary referent, the eucharistic layer is secondary and perhaps unnecessary; both Catholic and Baptist interpreters find this position unsatisfying.

synthesis: The eucharistic reading has strong patristic support (Ignatius, Cyril of Alexandria) and fits the escalating physicality of Jesus' language (from 'bread' to 'flesh' to 'eat/chew'). The metaphorical-faith reading notes that Jesus himself explains his words spiritually (6:63: 'the Spirit gives life; the flesh counts for nothing') and that the discourse culminates in faith language (6:69). The 'both' reading recognizes that John's Gospel characteristically operates on multiple levels — the discourse is about faith, which the Eucharist later enacts sacramentally.

title: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?
question: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?

positions.label: From the believer
positions.argument: "Whoever believes in me, as Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within them." The believer becomes a source of life for others through the indwelling Spirit.

positions.label: From Jesus
positions.proponents: Some scholars (Origen, some modern interpreters), supported by punctuation
positions.argument: Punctuate vv.37-38 as: "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me, and let the one who believes in me drink. As Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within him [Jesus]." The "him" refers to Jesus; the Spirit flows from the glorified Christ.

title: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?
question: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?

positions.label: Full divine name claim — eternal deity
positions.proponents: Most evangelical scholars, MacArthur, Carson, Keener
positions.argument: The egō eimi without predicate, the deliberate grammatical contrast (aorist genesthai vs present eimi), and the immediate stoning attempt (for blasphemy) all confirm a divine name claim. This is not merely pre-existence but eternal self-existent being.

positions.label: Pre-existence, not necessarily full deity
positions.argument: The statement asserts Jesus existed before Abraham; the "I am" is the natural Greek form for "I exist." The stoning may be for the whole discourse, not this specific phrase. This reading requires underweighting both the grammar and the opponents' response.

title: What is the purpose of the man's congenital blindness?
question: Does God cause suffering to display his glory, or does Jesus reject the premise of retributive theology?
context: When the disciples ask about a man born blind — 'who sinned, this man or his parents?' (John 9:2) — Jesus responds: 'Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him' (9:3). The answer is theologically explosive. Does Jesus mean God caused the blindness specifically so it could be healed (instrumental suffering)? Or is he rejecting the sin-suffering connection entirely and reframing the situation (not 'this happened so that' but 'but let the works of God be displayed')? The Greek hina clause is ambiguous.

positions.label: Divine purpose: revelation
positions.argument: God ordained the blindness for this purpose: that the works of God might be displayed. This does not make God the author of evil but means that God in his sovereignty uses suffering for redemptive ends.
positions.strengths: The grammar supports a purpose clause: 'so that' (hina) the works of God might be displayed; parallels John 11:4 where Lazarus's illness is 'for God's glory'; consistent with John's theology that everything serves the revelation of Jesus' identity; God's sovereignty encompasses suffering.
positions.weaknesses: Implies God caused decades of blindness for a single healing moment; creates a troubling theodicy; can be read as instrumentalizing human suffering for divine display; most disabled people are not healed.

positions.label: Natural misfortune repurposed
positions.argument: Jesus is not saying God caused the blindness but that God can use the blindness. The "so that" is consequential rather than purposive: given the blindness, the works of God will be displayed. This reading softens the providential claim.
positions.strengths: Jesus rejects the causal question entirely ('neither this man nor his parents sinned'); the blindness has natural causes, but Jesus repurposes the situation for revelation; separates God's response to suffering from God's causation of it; avoids the instrumental theodicy.
positions.weaknesses: The hina clause grammatically supports purpose, not merely result; this reading softens what the text appears to say; Jesus does not explicitly deny divine involvement in the man's condition.

positions.label: Eschatological purpose only
positions.argument: The man represents "the world" generically; the healing represents the eschatological restoration. The narrative is primarily symbolic rather than historical.
positions.strengths: The healing is a sign of God's kingdom breaking into the present age; the man's condition represents the spiritual blindness Jesus came to heal (9:39); the purpose is eschatological — the revelation of Jesus as 'the light of the world' (9:5) — not a commentary on individual suffering.
positions.weaknesses: Does not fully address the individual theodicy question; the man's actual physical suffering is not merely a symbol; risks spiritualizing a real person's real disability.

synthesis: The divine-purpose reading is the traditional interpretation and has strong grammatical support (hina as purpose clause). However, it creates a difficult theodicy — God deliberately causing blindness for a future healing. The repurposing reading avoids this problem by separating causation from response. The eschatological reading shifts focus from individual suffering to the in-breaking of God's kingdom. Most scholars acknowledge the tension: Jesus clearly rejects the retributive theology ('who sinned?') while affirming that God's purposes operate through suffering — without fully resolving how.

title: "I and the Father are one" — unity of essence or unity of purpose?
question: Does 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30) assert ontological unity (same divine essence) or functional unity (shared purpose)?
context: Jesus' declaration 'I and the Father are one' (hen, neuter — 'one thing,' not heis, masculine — 'one person') provokes an immediate attempt to stone him for blasphemy (10:31-33).
